Option A is incorrect because safety science acknowledges that human error cannot be entirely eliminated. Option C is incorrect because while it aims to maximize recovery, it cannot guarantee 100% success in all cases. Option D is incorrect because patient safety is a clinical and systemic discipline, not a purely legal one. Option E is incorrect because hospital administration is a separate function, though it supports safety.

options.A: The elimination of all human error in clinical practice.
options.B: A discipline that applies safety science to achieve a trustworthy system.
options.C: A system that ensures 100% recovery from all surgical complications.
options.D: The legal framework used to prosecute medical negligence.
options.E: The administrative process of managing hospital budgets.

questions.question: The conceptual model of patient safety divides healthcare into four domains. Which of the following is one of these specific domains?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the model by Emanuel et al. includes the infrastructure of systems for therapeutic interventions as one of its four main domains. Option A is incorrect as the model focuses on healthcare delivery rather than the legal system. Option B is incorrect because financial profitability is not a core domain of this safety model. Option C is incorrect because academic ranking is irrelevant to the functional domains of the safety system. Option D is incorrect because while location affects access, it is not a defined domain in this specific conceptual framework.

options.A: The legal regulations governing medical malpractice.
options.B: The financial profitability of the healthcare facility.
options.C: The academic ranking of the medical institution.
options.D: The geographical location of the hospital.
options.E: The infrastructure of systems for therapeutic interventions.

questions.question: How is "Negligence" distinguished from "Professional Misconduct" in a medico-legal context?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because negligence is defined as a failure to exercise expected skill or care, whereas misconduct is a significant departure from the standard of care expected of a professional. Option A is incorrect because negligence is often unintentional (omission). Option B is incorrect because both terms apply to all healthcare professionals. Option D is incorrect because both can be viewed through a systems lens, but they are legal definitions of behavior, not system categories. Option E is incorrect because negligence often results in harm, and misconduct does not necessarily result in death.

options.A: Negligence is always intentional, while misconduct is accidental.
options.B: Negligence only applies to doctors, while misconduct applies to nurses.
options.C: Negligence involves a failure of skill, while misconduct is a departure from standards.
options.D: Negligence is a system failure, while misconduct is an active failure.
options.E: Negligence results in no harm, while misconduct always results in death.

questions.question: What is the primary danger of "Hindsight Bias" in the investigation of medical errors?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because hindsight bias is the tendency to see an event as having been predictable after it has already occurred, which can lead to unfair blaming. Option B is incorrect because while it may affect the view of the provider, it doesn't inherently ignore the patient. Option C is incorrect because it relates to perception of causality, not financial auditing. Option D is incorrect because one can still use the model, though the bias might distort the analysis of the "holes." Option E is incorrect because the bias relies on knowing the outcome, not forgetting it.

options.A: It makes the error seem more predictable than it actually was.
options.B: It makes the investigator ignore the patient's perspective.
options.C: It leads to an overestimation of the economic cost of the error.
options.D: It prevents the use of the Swiss Cheese Model.
options.E: It causes the investigator to forget the details of the incident.

questions.question: A surgical team has stopped performing the final "time-out" check because they have not had a wrong-site surgery in five years. Which concept best explains this behavior?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because Diane Vaughan described the "normalization of deviance" as a process where risky assessments or violations become the accepted rule through repeated negotiation in an imperfect environment. Option A is incorrect because optimizing violations are driven by personal gain or thrills, not necessarily a gradual erosion of standards. Option B is incorrect because the team knows the rule but chooses to ignore it; it is not a lack of knowledge. Option C is incorrect because a mistake is an error in the plan, whereas this is a deliberate deviation from a known safe protocol. Option E is incorrect because necessary violations are usually driven by time pressure or lack of resources, not a false sense of security.

questions.question: Research shows that prolonged work and sleep deprivation can impair performance to a level equivalent to what blood alcohol concentration?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the text states that prolonged work produces the same deterioration in performance as a blood alcohol level of 0.05 mmol/l. Option A is incorrect as it is a negligible level. Option C is incorrect as it is the legal driving limit in some countries but higher than the specific figure mentioned in the text. Option D is incorrect as it represents a much higher level of intoxication. Option E is incorrect as it represents extreme intoxication not cited in the curriculum.

questions.question: A patient notices that the nurse is about to hang a bag of medication that looks different from the one they received yesterday and speaks up. This is an example of the patient acting as which component of the safety system?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the text notes that patients are a "second pair of eyes" and can act as a warning to the team, effectively serving as a layer of defense in the system. Option A is incorrect because latent conditions are systemic flaws that lead to errors, not safety checks. Option B is incorrect because the second victim is the healthcare provider traumatized by an error. Option C is incorrect because an active failure is the error itself, which the patient helped prevent. Option D is incorrect because a root cause is the underlying reason for a failure, not the mechanism that stops it.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Which model of human error assumes that if health-care workers are sufficiently trained and work hard enough, mistakes can be entirely avoided?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the perfectibility model is based on the premise that error-free performance is possible through individual effort, vigilance, and training, essentially ignoring human biological limitations. Option A is incorrect because the systems model acknowledges that errors are inevitable and focuses on organizational design to catch them. Option C is incorrect because High Reliability Organizations (HROs) actually anticipate failure rather than assuming it can be avoided by effort alone. Option D is incorrect because human factors science explicitly rejects the idea that humans can be perfect and instead designs for fallibility. Option E is incorrect because resilience refers to the ability to recover from unexpected threats, not the avoidance of all errors.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A hospital administrator wants to redesign the medication room to reduce the cognitive load on nurses. Which discipline provides the scientific basis for this intervention?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because human factors engineering (or ergonomics) is the science of designing systems, tools, and environments to match human capabilities and limitations, specifically to reduce errors. Option A is incorrect because clinical pathology deals with the laboratory analysis of bodily fluids for diagnosis. Option B is incorrect because medical ethics focuses on moral principles and decision-making in patient care. Option C is incorrect because hospital sociology examines social interactions and structures but does not focus on environmental design for error reduction. Option D is incorrect because health economics studies the efficiency and value of health-care delivery systems.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: During a ward round, a medical student notices that the consultant is about to prescribe a drug to which the patient has a documented allergy. The student mentions it once, but the consultant is distracted and continues writing. According to the "Two-Challenge Rule," what is the student's next appropriate action?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the Two-Challenge Rule requires team members to voice their concern at least twice if the initial assertion is ignored, with the second challenge being more assertive to ensure the message is heard. Option A is incorrect because waiting for another professional to catch the error abdicates the student's responsibility for immediate patient safety. Option B is incorrect because documentation does not prevent the immediate harm of the incorrect prescription. Option C is incorrect because legal reporting is a later administrative step and does not address the immediate clinical risk. Option E is incorrect because assuming infallibility in the face of a known risk is a violation of patient safety principles.

options.A: Wait for the pharmacist to catch the error during the dispensing phase.
options.B: Document the consultant's refusal to listen in the patient's progress notes.
options.C: Immediately inform the hospital's legal department about the incident.
options.D: Restate the concern clearly, providing explicit support for the worry.
options.E: Assume the consultant has a clinical reason for overriding the allergy.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: In the context of organizational accidents, what term describes the process where experts in an imperfect environment gradually accept risky behaviors as the standard operating procedure?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because "normalization of deviance" refers to the process where people within an organization become so accustomed to a deviation (like skipping a safety step) that they no longer see it as a risk. Option B is incorrect because hindsight bias is the tendency to see an event as having been predictable after it has already occurred. Option C is incorrect because cognitive dissonance is the mental discomfort experienced by a person who holds contradictory beliefs. Option D is incorrect because attribution error refers to the tendency to overemphasize personal characteristics and ignore situational factors in judging others' behavior. Option E is incorrect because psychological safety is the belief that one will not be punished for speaking up about errors.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A surgical team is performing a complex procedure. The hospital has recently cut staffing levels, and the surgeon is operating after a 24-hour shift. During the final stages, the surgeon accidentally nicks a major vessel. According to Reason's Swiss Cheese Model, which element represents the "Active Failure"?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because active failures are the errors or violations committed by those at the "sharp end" of the system (clinicians) that have immediate adverse effects. Option A is incorrect because management decisions are latent conditions that reside within the system's structure. Option B is incorrect because fatigue is a latent condition or error-producing factor that predisposes an individual to make a mistake. Option C is incorrect because the absence of protocols is a latent organizational failure. Option E is incorrect because a poor safety culture is a latent condition that influences behavior over time but is not the immediate act causing harm.

options.A: The decision by management to reduce the number of scrub nurses.
options.B: The surgeon's physical state of exhaustion from the long shift.
options.C: The lack of a standardized protocol for emergency vessel repair.
options.D: The specific act of the surgeon nicking the blood vessel.
options.E: The hospital's culture of not reporting near-misses in the OR.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A doctor tells a nurse, "Please administer 0.5 mg of Epinephrine." The nurse responds, "I will administer 0.5 mg of Epinephrine." The doctor confirms, "That is correct." Which communication technique is being used?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a check-back is a closed-loop communication technique where the receiver repeats the message to ensure it was heard correctly, and the sender confirms it. Option B is incorrect because ISBAR is a structured framework for communicating critical patient information (Introduction, Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation). Option C is incorrect because a call-out is used to inform all team members simultaneously during an emergency. Option D is incorrect because a handoff is the transfer of responsibility and information during transitions of care. Option E is incorrect because the DESC script is a tool used for conflict resolution.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Which characteristic of a High Reliability Organization (HRO) involves acknowledging and planning for the possibility of system failure due to the high-risk nature of activities?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because preoccupation with failure means that HROs treat any lapse as a symptom that something is wrong with the system, constantly looking for potential flaws. Option A is incorrect because sensitivity to operations refers to paying attention to the frontline work and real-time conditions. Option B is incorrect because commitment to resilience is the ability to contain and recover from errors. Option C is incorrect because deference to expertise means allowing the person with the most knowledge of a situation to make decisions, regardless of rank. Option D is incorrect because reluctance to simplify involves digging deeper into the complexities of why things go wrong rather than accepting simple explanations.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: When an unexpected adverse event occurs, people naturally tend to look for a specific individual to blame to make sense of the situation. Which psychological concept explains this behavior?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because attribution theory describes how individuals explain the causes of events, often leading to blaming personal faults over situational factors (the fundamental attribution error). Option A is incorrect because confirmation bias is the tendency to search for information that supports one's preconceptions. Option C is incorrect because groupthink is the practice of making decisions as a group in a way that discourages individual responsibility or dissenting views. Option D is incorrect because the availability heuristic is a mental shortcut that relies on immediate examples that come to mind. Option E is incorrect because the anchoring effect is the tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information offered.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A nurse is concerned that a senior surgeon is not following the sterile scrubbing protocol. The nurse decides to use the DESC script to resolve this conflict. What should the "S" in DESC represent in this interaction?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because in the DESC script (Describe, Express, Suggest, Consequences), the "S" stands for "Suggest other alternatives and seek agreement." Option A is incorrect because stating consequences is the "C" part of the script. Option B is incorrect because seeking a supervisor is an escalation step, not part of the DESC mnemonic itself. Option C is incorrect because the script focuses on the specific current behavior, not past history. Option E is incorrect because while showing policy might be part of the description, it is not the primary definition of the "S" component.

options.A: Stating the safety consequences of the behavior.
options.B: Seeking a supervisor to intervene immediately.
options.D: Suggesting an alternative or seeking agreement on a solution.
options.E: Showing the surgeon the written hospital policy.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Human factors research suggests that relying on a person's ability to remain constantly alert during repetitive tasks is an ineffective safety strategy. What is this human limitation called?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because vigilance is the ability to maintain concentrated attention over prolonged periods; humans are naturally poor at this, especially during repetitive tasks. Option A is incorrect because working memory capacity refers to the amount of information one can hold in mind at once. Option B is incorrect because sensory overload occurs when senses experience over-stimulation from the environment. Option C is incorrect because cognitive bias refers to systematic patterns of deviation from rationality in judgment. Option D is incorrect because motor skill decay refers to the loss of physical coordination over time without practice.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: During a cardiac arrest resuscitation, the team leader shouts, "Airway status?" and the respiratory therapist immediately responds, "Airway clear!" Which communication strategy is being utilized?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because a call-out is a strategy used to communicate critical information simultaneously to all team members during an emergency, helping everyone anticipate the next steps. Option A is incorrect because ISBAR is a structured report format, not a rapid-fire emergency exchange. Option C is incorrect because a handoff involves transferring responsibility for a patient between teams. Option D is incorrect because the two-challenge rule is for voicing concerns about safety breaches. Option E is incorrect because CUS is a graded assertiveness tool (Concerned, Uncomfortable, Safety issue).

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: In High Reliability Organizations (HROs), what does the characteristic "Commitment to Resilience" specifically refer to?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because resilience in HROs is the ability to recognize that things will go wrong and having the systems in place to contain the error and recover quickly. Option B is incorrect because HROs recognize that humans cannot work indefinitely without error and manage fatigue accordingly. Option C is incorrect because HROs favor a "just culture" over a punitive one to encourage reporting. Option D is incorrect because HROs rely on human expertise and "sensitivity to operations" rather than just automation. Option E is incorrect because while HROs use engineering principles, they value multidisciplinary expertise from all staff.

options.A: The capacity to identify and contain unexpected threats before they cause harm.
options.B: The ability of staff to work long hours without making errors.
options.C: The implementation of strict disciplinary actions for every mistake.
options.D: The use of advanced technology to replace all human decision-making.
options.E: The requirement for all staff to have a background in engineering.

questions.question: According to the modern conceptual model, patient safety is defined not just as a set of actions, but as which specific characteristic of a healthcare system?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because patient safety is defined as an attribute of healthcare systems that minimizes the incidence and impact of adverse events while maximizing recovery from them. Option A is incorrect because while administration manages safety, safety itself is a system property. Option B is incorrect because although professionals have obligations, the definition focuses on the system's nature. Option C is incorrect because safety is the framework that leads to outcomes, not the outcome itself. Option E is incorrect because legal standards are external benchmarks, whereas safety is an internal system attribute.

questions.question: Beyond the human suffering involved, what is the primary economic justification for prioritizing patient safety in global health policy?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because studies show that adverse events cost countries billions annually due to prolonged hospital stays, lost income, and legal expenses. Option A is incorrect because safety often requires better, not less, technology. Option C is incorrect because safety usually requires more coordinated, specialized teams. Option D is incorrect because insurance remains necessary even in safe systems. Option E is incorrect because safety aims to reduce total costs for all payers, not just shift the burden.

options.A: It reduces the need for expensive medical technology.
options.B: It minimizes the significant costs associated with additional hospitalization and litigation.
options.C: It allows hospitals to employ fewer highly specialized staff.
options.D: It eliminates the requirement for professional indemnity insurance.
options.E: It shifts the financial burden of care from the government to private insurers.

questions.question: A hospital pharmacy incorrectly stocks 10% phenol vials next to standard contrast medium. A busy nurse picks up the phenol, and the surgical team injects it into a patient. In the "Swiss Cheese Model," the incorrect stocking of the medication is classified as:
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because latent conditions are systemic flaws (like poor storage or design) created by people at the management or administrative level that lie dormant until they combine with active failures. Option A is incorrect because the nurse picking up the wrong vial is the active failure. Option B is incorrect because a routine violation is a deliberate deviation from rules, not a storage error. Option D is incorrect because optimizing violations involve taking risks for personal gain. Option E is incorrect because psychological precursors refer to the mental state of the worker (e.g., stress), not the physical environment.

questions.question: When investigating a clinical error, a team uses the "Five Whys" technique. What is the primary goal of this specific investigative approach?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the "Five Whys" is designed to drill down through layers of causality to find the systemic root causes rather than stopping at human error. Option A is incorrect because the technique avoids individual blame. Option B is incorrect because it is a safety tool, not a legal investigative tool. Option C is incorrect because while it helps in reporting, its primary goal is internal improvement. Option E is incorrect because it is used for learning, not for punishment or discipline.

options.A: To identify the individual most responsible for the mistake.
options.B: To determine if the error meets the legal definition of negligence.
options.C: To satisfy the reporting requirements of external regulatory bodies.
options.D: To move the focus from the immediate human error to the underlying system flaws.
options.E: To provide a structured format for a formal disciplinary hearing.

questions.question: A surgeon performs a procedure correctly according to the established plan, but the patient suffers a complication because the plan itself was based on an outdated clinical guideline. This scenario is best categorized as:
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because a mistake is a deficiency in the judgmental or inferential processes; the action goes as planned, but the plan is wrong. Option A is incorrect because a slip is an observational or execution failure (e.g., picking up the wrong tool). Option B is incorrect because a lapse is a memory failure. Option C is incorrect because a violation is a deliberate deviation from a known rule. Option E is incorrect because negligence usually implies a failure to exercise reasonable care, whereas here the surgeon followed a plan they believed was correct.

questions.question: High Reliability Organizations (HROs), such as nuclear power plants, maintain safety by being "preoccupied with failure." How is this characteristic applied in a healthcare setting?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because HROs view small lapses or near misses as indicators of system vulnerability, allowing them to fix problems before a catastrophe occurs. Option A is incorrect because HROs rely on trust and reporting, not an assumption of poor ethics. Option B is incorrect because they look at all failures, especially those that could lead to major harm. Option D is incorrect because HROs encourage reporting through a non-punitive culture. Option E is incorrect because HROs manage complexity rather than simply avoiding it.

options.A: By assuming that all staff will eventually act unethically.
options.B: By focusing exclusively on the most common, minor errors.
options.C: By treating every "near miss" as a symptom of a potential systemic breakdown.
options.D: By punishing any staff member who reports a minor mistake.
options.E: By reducing the complexity of treatments to avoid any risk.

questions.question: According to the WHO International Classification for Patient Safety, how is an "Adverse Reaction" distinguished from other types of harm?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the WHO defines an adverse reaction as harm resulting from a justified action in the right context, distinguishing it from errors. Option A is incorrect because violations are deliberate deviations, not reactions. Option C is incorrect because reactions can occur even at the correct dose. Option D is incorrect because side effects are known pharmacological properties, whereas adverse reactions are defined by the context of the event. Option E is incorrect because equipment failure is a different category of incident.

options.A: It results from a deliberate violation of protocol.
options.B: It is an unexpected harm resulting from a justified action where the correct process was followed.
options.C: It is a harm that occurs only when a medication is administered at the wrong dose.
options.D: It is a predictable side effect that was explained to the patient during consent.
options.E: It is a harm caused by faulty medical equipment rather than human error.

questions.question: In the context of the "Challenger" space shuttle disaster, the term "Normalization of Deviance" was coined. What does this concept warn healthcare professionals about?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because normalization of deviance occurs when people become so accustomed to cutting corners or ignoring minor safety rules that they no longer see them as risks. Option A is incorrect because this phenomenon often affects experienced staff who have "successfully" bypassed rules before. Option C is incorrect because it refers to behavioral culture, not financial cost. Option D is incorrect because that describes the "second victim" phenomenon. Option E is incorrect because while communication is a factor, the term specifically refers to the erosion of safety standards.

options.A: The tendency for new staff to make more errors than experienced staff.
options.B: The gradual acceptance of small rule violations as a standard way of working.
options.C: The high cost of implementing new safety technologies.
options.D: The emotional distress felt by clinicians after a major error.
options.E: The difficulty of communicating across different medical specialties.

questions.question: A patient on a "nil by mouth" order for surgery is accidentally served a meal by a new catering staff member who was not briefed on the patient's status. This incident demonstrates that patient safety is dependent on which group in the TeamSTEPPS framework?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because ancillary services (like catering, cleaning, or laundry) provide task-specific support that is vital to the patient's safety, even if they aren't direct clinical providers. Option A is incorrect because the core team consists of direct clinical providers like doctors and nurses. Option B is incorrect because contingency teams are for emergencies like cardiac arrests. Option C is incorrect because coordinating teams manage operational resources. Option E is incorrect because administration sets the overall policy but does not deliver the specific service in this case.

questions.question: When using the "IM SAFE" mnemonic for self-assessment before a shift, what does the "E" represent?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the "E" in the aviation-derived IM SAFE tool stands for Emotion, reminding clinicians to check if they are emotionally fit to provide safe care. Option A is incorrect because experience is a qualification, not a transient state of fitness. Option B is incorrect because ethics is a constant requirement, not a daily check of physical/mental state. Option C is incorrect because the tool focuses on the individual's internal state, not the external environment. Option D is incorrect because equipment is a system factor, not a personal fitness factor.

questions.question: Why does the increasing specialization of healthcare professionals, while beneficial for treatment, paradoxically increase the risk to patient safety?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because as care is split among more specialists, the number of transitions (hand-overs) increases, and each transition is a high-risk point for communication failure. Option A is incorrect because specialization usually increases the number of staff involved. Option B is incorrect because while complexity is high, the primary safety risk is the communication between providers. Option D is incorrect because there is no direct evidence that specialization drives patients to traditional healers. Option E is incorrect because specialization actually increases the need for guidelines to coordinate care.

options.A: It leads to a decrease in the overall number of healthcare workers.
options.B: It makes it more difficult for patients to understand their own diagnosis.
options.C: It creates more "hand-over" points where critical information can be lost.
options.D: It encourages patients to seek care from unqualified traditional healers.
options.E: It reduces the need for evidence-based clinical guidelines.

questions.question: A healthcare system is described as "resilient" when it can maintain safe operations despite unexpected threats. Which of the following best exemplifies systemic resilience?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because resilience is the ability of a system (and its people) to proactively seek out threats and contain them or adapt to maintain safety when things go wrong. Option A is incorrect because a blame culture destroys the reporting needed for resilience. Option C is incorrect because avoiding complexity is not the same as being resilient within it. Option D is incorrect because zero reporting often indicates a "pathological" culture that hides errors, not a resilient one. Option E is incorrect because relying on memory is a known human factors vulnerability, the opposite of a resilient system design.

options.A: A hospital that fires any staff member involved in a medication error.
options.B: A surgical team that successfully adapts when a piece of equipment fails mid-procedure.
options.C: A clinic that refuses to treat patients with complex, multi-organ diseases.
options.D: A department that has never reported an adverse event in ten years.
options.E: A facility that relies solely on the memory of its most senior consultants.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Which of the following terms is used interchangeably with "human factors" when describing the interaction between individuals and their work environment?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because ergonomics is the established science that studies the relationship between humans, their tools, and their environment, and is synonymous with human factors in safety literature. Option A is incorrect as psychosocial dynamics refers specifically to the influence of social factors on an individual's mind or behavior. Option C is incorrect because bioengineering is the application of engineering principles to biology and medicine, often for creating artificial organs or prosthetics. Option D is incorrect as cognitive therapy is a form of psychological treatment for mental health disorders. Option E is incorrect because organizational behavior is the study of how people act within groups, which is only one subset of human factors.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A hospital administrator insists that if doctors and nurses are sufficiently trained and work with enough care, medical errors can be entirely avoided. Which model of safety does this viewpoint represent?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the "perfectibility model" is the traditional approach that assumes errors are the result of individual failure, lack of hard work, or insufficient training. Option A is incorrect as the systems approach focuses on the environment and processes rather than individual perfection. Option B is incorrect because High Reliability Organizations (HROs) acknowledge that failure is always possible and plan for it. Option C is incorrect as the Swiss Cheese Model illustrates how multiple system failures align to cause an accident. Option E is incorrect because the resilience framework focuses on an organization's ability to adapt and recover from unexpected threats.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A medical student observes a senior registrar about to perform a procedure on the wrong limb. The student asks a clarifying question, but the registrar dismisses the concern. According to the "two-challenge rule," what is the student's required next action?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the "two-challenge rule" mandates that if an initial assertion is ignored, the team member must voice the concern at least a second time, providing support for the concern to ensure the other person has heard and understood. Option B is incorrect as reporting to an ethics committee is a long-term administrative action, not an immediate safety intervention. Option C is incorrect because waiting for the outcome allows the error to reach the patient. Option D is incorrect as the rule is designed to empower juniors to challenge authority in the interest of safety. Option E is incorrect because the rule focuses on graded communication before physical intervention or moving up the chain of command.

options.A: Restate the concern with supporting evidence to ensure it is acknowledged
options.B: Immediately report the registrar to the hospital's ethics committee
options.C: Document the disagreement in the patient's chart and wait for the outcome
options.D: Accept the registrar's authority as the senior decision-maker
options.E: Physically intervene to stop the registrar from touching the patient

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A healthcare professional deliberately deviates from a standard operating protocol to achieve a personal goal, such as finishing a shift early or seeking a "thrill" from taking a risk. How is this behavior classified in human factors research?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because "optimizing violations" are those where a person is motivated by personal goals, such as greed, thrills, or convenience, rather than the requirements of the task. Option A is incorrect as routine violations are common, tolerated deviations often caused by a desire to get the job done. Option B is incorrect because an error is an unintentional failure, whereas a violation is a deliberate deviation. Option C is incorrect as necessary violations are those forced by organizational failures or time pressure to complete a task. Option D is incorrect because a slip is an unintentional action where the plan was correct but the execution was flawed.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A patient is accidentally administered a caustic substance instead of a contrast agent. During the subsequent investigation, the team uses the "Five Whys" technique. They discover the pharmacy supplied the wrong vial because the labels looked identical. What is the primary purpose of asking "Why?" for the fifth time in this process?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the "Five Whys" technique is designed to move past superficial symptoms of a problem to reach the underlying systemic or organizational factors that allowed the error to occur. Option A is incorrect as the technique is a systems-thinking tool, not a tool for assigning individual blame. Option B is incorrect because the focus is on the process of care, not the patient's physiology. Option D is incorrect as economic calculation is a separate administrative function. Option E is incorrect because while it supports a safety culture, its primary goal is root cause identification for improvement, not just satisfying a reporting requirement.

options.A: To identify which specific pharmacist should be held legally accountable
options.B: To determine if the patient’s underlying condition contributed to the harm
options.C: To uncover deep-seated organizational or systemic root causes
options.D: To calculate the exact economic cost of the adverse event
options.E: To satisfy the hospital's requirement for a "blame-free" report

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Following a serious medication error that resulted in patient harm, the nurse involved experiences severe anxiety, guilt, and a loss of professional confidence. What term does human factors literature use to describe this individual?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the "second victim" concept, introduced by Albert Wu, refers to healthcare providers who are emotionally traumatized by an adverse event in which they were involved. Option A is incorrect as a latent failure is a hidden flaw in the system design. Option C is incorrect because an active failure is the error itself committed at the point of care. Option D is incorrect as a safety advocate is someone who promotes safety protocols. Option E is incorrect because a resilient practitioner is one who can adapt to stress, but the term does not specifically describe the trauma of being involved in an error.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Human factors research indicates that the relationship between stress and human performance is not linear. Which of the following best describes this relationship?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because human performance follows a curve where moderate stress can enhance focus, but both excessive stress and very low stress (leading to boredom/inattention) result in decreased performance. Option A is incorrect as high stress eventually leads to panic and breakdown of cognitive function. Option B is incorrect because low stress often leads to a lack of vigilance and boredom. Option C is incorrect as stress is a well-documented factor in human error. Option E is incorrect because all humans, regardless of experience, are subject to the physiological effects of stress.

options.A: Performance improves indefinitely as stress levels increase
options.B: Performance is highest when stress is completely absent
options.C: Stress has no measurable impact on the accuracy of clinical tasks
options.D: Both very high and very low levels of stress can impair performance
options.E: Stress only affects inexperienced students, not senior clinicians

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A nurse is concerned that a patient is being discharged too early. She uses the DESC script to resolve the conflict with the attending physician. She has already Described the situation and Expressed her concerns. What is her next step in this specific communication tool?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the DESC script stands for Describe, Express, Suggest, and Consequences; therefore, after expressing concerns, the next step is to Suggest alternatives and seek consensus. Option A is incorrect as stating the consequences is the final step of the DESC script. Option B is incorrect because the DESC script is a constructive consensus-building tool, not an immediate escalation to high-level management. Option C is incorrect as documentation is a separate administrative task, not a step in the DESC communication tool. Option D is incorrect because a "Call-out" is used to share critical information simultaneously in emergencies, not for conflict resolution.

options.A: State the Consequences of the current plan
options.B: Demand an immediate consultation with the Chief of Staff
options.C: Document the physician's refusal in the legal record
options.D: Perform a "Call-out" to alert the rest of the ward

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: In the context of team communication, which technique involves the recipient repeating back the information received to ensure the sender's message was understood correctly?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a "check-back" is a closed-loop communication technique where the receiver accepts the message and provides feedback, and the sender double-checks for accuracy. Option B is incorrect as a call-out is used to inform all team members simultaneously during an emergency. Option C is incorrect because a huddle is a short, ad-hoc meeting to re-establish situational awareness. Option D is incorrect as a briefing is a meeting held before an activity to assign roles and set expectations. Option E is incorrect because a debriefing is held after an event to review performance and lessons learned.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: High Reliability Organizations (HROs) are characterized by a "commitment to resilience." What does this specifically mean in a clinical setting?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because commitment to resilience in HROs refers to the ability to anticipate, detect, and contain unexpected threats before they result in a catastrophe. Option A is incorrect as HROs favor a learning culture over a punitive one. Option B is incorrect because HROs value "deference to expertise" rather than just seniority. Option D is incorrect as while HROs use standardization, they recognize that complex systems still require human adaptability and resilience. Option E is incorrect because HROs maintain structures but encourage a "flat" communication style regarding safety concerns.

options.A: Punishing every minor error to ensure they are never repeated
options.B: Relying on the most senior doctor to make all safety decisions
options.C: Proactively seeking out threats and containing them before harm occurs
options.D: Standardizing every single task so that human judgment is unnecessary
options.E: Eliminating all hierarchies so that every staff member has equal power

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A surgical team has stopped using the mandatory "Time Out" checklist because they have performed the procedure hundreds of times without an error. They now believe the checklist is an unnecessary waste of time. Which human factors concept does this illustrate?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because "normalization of deviance" (a term from Diane Vaughan's analysis of the Challenger crash) occurs when people become so accustomed to a violation that it no longer feels like a risk, especially when no immediate bad outcome occurs. Option A is incorrect as psychological safety is the belief that one can speak up without being punished. Option C is incorrect because sensitivity to operations is an HRO characteristic of paying close attention to front-line work. Option D is incorrect as deference to expertise means listening to the person with the most knowledge, not ignoring safety rules. Option E is incorrect because hindsight bias is the tendency to see an event as predictable after it has already happened.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: In the "I PASS the BATON" handoff tool, which is used to ensure the safe transfer of patient responsibility, what does the final "N" stand for?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the "N" in "I PASS the BATON" stands for "Next," which refers to what will happen next, including anticipated changes and contingency plans. Option A is incorrect as "Notes" are part of the background or assessment but not the specific "N" in this mnemonic. Option B is incorrect because the tool is multi-professional and not specific to one discipline. Option C is incorrect as "Notice" is not a standard part of this specific handoff tool. Option E is incorrect because the patient's name is covered under the "P" (Patient) or "I" (Introduction) sections.

questions.question: Which traditional healthcare mindset assumes that errors can be avoided if professionals simply work harder and receive more training?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the perfectibility model is the traditional view that assumes if healthcare workers care enough and are well-trained, they will not make mistakes. Option A is incorrect because the systems approach focuses on the environment and processes rather than individual effort. Option C is incorrect because human factors engineering acknowledges human fallibility and designs systems to accommodate it. Option D is incorrect because high reliability theory focuses on organizational preoccupation with failure to maintain safety. Option E is incorrect because resilience engineering focuses on the ability of a system to adapt to changing conditions.

questions.question: A hospital's catering staff inadvertently delivers a solid meal to a patient who is on a "nil by mouth" order for surgery. In the TeamSTEPPS framework, which group does this staff belong to?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because ancillary services consist of staff who provide task-specific support services, such as catering or cleaning, which are vital for patient safety. Option A is incorrect because the core team consists of direct care providers like doctors and nurses. Option B is incorrect because the coordinating team manages day-to-day operational resources. Option C is incorrect because contingency teams are formed for emergent events like cardiac arrests. Option D is incorrect because administration refers to executive leadership and policy makers.

questions.question: A patient is prescribed a standard dose of an antibiotic for a confirmed infection. Despite the physician following all evidence-based protocols and the nurse administering the drug correctly, the patient develops an unexpected and severe skin rash. According to the WHO International Classification for Patient Safety, how is this incident categorized?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because an adverse reaction is defined as unexpected harm resulting from a justified action where the correct process was followed. Option B is incorrect because the incident was not avoidable as all protocols were followed. Option C is incorrect because a violation involves a deliberate deviation from a rule or procedure. Option D is incorrect because a near miss is an incident that does not reach the patient. Option E is incorrect because a no-harm incident reaches the patient but results in no discernable harm, whereas a rash is a form of harm.

questions.question: What is the most effective method for a student to verify a patient's identity before taking a blood sample?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because a three-way check involves matching the patient's open-ended response (e.g., "What is your name?") with the ID band and the request form. Option A is incorrect because bed charts can be misplaced or belong to a previous occupant. Option B is incorrect because patients may agree with the wrong name due to confusion or hearing difficulties. Option D is incorrect because verbal confirmation alone is prone to communication errors. Option E is incorrect because room numbers are not reliable identifiers as patients frequently move.

options.A: Checking the name on the bed chart
options.B: Asking the patient, "Are you Mr. Smith?"
options.C: Performing a three-way check with an open-ended question
options.D: Relying on the nurse's verbal confirmation
options.E: Matching the room number to the request form

questions.question: A senior surgeon decides to allow an inexperienced medical student to perform a complex procedure unsupervised because the surgeon is in a hurry to attend to a private clinic. Which type of violation does this represent?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because optimizing violations occur when a person is motivated by personal goals, such as saving time for personal interests or financial gain. Option A is incorrect because routine violations are common, tolerated deviations like skipping hand hygiene. Option B is incorrect because necessary violations are driven by time-poor environments where the goal is still patient care. Option C is incorrect because a system failure is an organizational breakdown, not a deliberate individual choice. Option E is incorrect because latent conditions are hidden flaws in the system design.

questions.question: During a busy night shift, a "Code Blue" is called for a patient in respiratory distress. A team of clinicians from various departments, who do not usually work together, assembles immediately to manage the resuscitation. According to the TeamSTEPPS framework, what type of team has been formed?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because contingency teams are formed for emergent or specific events and are drawn from various core teams to respond to crises. Option A is incorrect because core teams are the stable groups directly involved in routine patient care. Option C is incorrect because support services manage the facility environment and logistics. Option D is incorrect because coordinating teams handle operational management and resource allocation. Option E is incorrect because ancillary teams provide specific support tasks like cleaning or catering.

questions.question: In the context of the "Challenger" shuttle disaster, Diane Vaughan described a process where experts gradually accept increasing levels of risk as a normal part of operations. What is this phenomenon called?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because normalization of deviance refers to the process where risky assessments and violations become the rule rather than the exception through continued negotiation. Option A is incorrect because hindsight bias is the tendency to see an event as predictable after it has occurred. Option B is incorrect because attribution theory relates to how people make sense of the causes of events. Option C is incorrect because active failures are errors made by front-line workers with immediate effects. Option D is incorrect because psychological safety is the ability to speak up without fear of retribution.

questions.question: In Reason's Swiss Cheese Model, what term describes organizational flaws such as inadequate staffing or poor equipment design that exist long before an accident?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because latent conditions are the result of management decisions or poor designs that stay hidden until they combine with other factors to cause an accident. Option B is incorrect because active failures are the immediate errors made by staff at the "sharp end." Option C is incorrect because safety barriers are the layers of defense designed to prevent harm. Option D is incorrect because optimizing violations are deliberate choices for personal gain. Option E is incorrect because sentinel events are the actual serious adverse outcomes.

questions.question: When a junior nurse notices a senior doctor about to administer the wrong medication, she uses the "Two-Challenge Rule." What should her first challenge be?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the first step of the two-challenge rule should be in the form of a question to allow the other person to reconsider their action. Option A is incorrect because a direct command is a later, more assertive step if the first challenges are ignored. Option B is incorrect because stating discomfort is part of the CUS mnemonic, not the first step of the two-challenge rule. Option D is incorrect because reporting to administration is a final escalation step. Option E is incorrect because the DESC script is used for conflict resolution, not immediate clinical intervention.

options.A: A direct command to stop the procedure
options.B: A statement of her personal discomfort
options.C: A question to clarify the situation
options.D: A report to the hospital administrator

questions.question: A medical student is assisting in the ICU and feels that the current ventilator settings for a patient are dangerously high. He says to the registrar, "I am concerned about these settings; I am uncomfortable with the patient's oxygen saturation, and I believe this is a safety issue." Which communication tool is the student using?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because CUS stands for "I am Concerned, I am Uncomfortable, this is a Safety issue," which is a graded way to stop a problematic activity. Option A is incorrect because ISBAR is used for structured handovers or referrals. Option B is incorrect because the two-challenge rule involves repeating a concern twice if ignored. Option C is incorrect because check-back is a closed-loop communication technique to verify orders. Option E is incorrect because call-out is used to share critical information with a whole team simultaneously during an emergency.

questions.question: Why is the patient considered the most "resilient" component of the healthcare system in the context of patient safety?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the text notes that many adverse events are avoided because patients get better despite system failures, acting as a resilient buffer. Option A is incorrect because while consent is a legal right, it does not define systemic resilience. Option C is incorrect because while patients have expertise in their experience, resilience in this context refers to the ability to withstand system errors. Option D is incorrect because reporting is usually the responsibility of the healthcare organization. Option E is incorrect because financial support is unrelated to the concept of safety resilience.

options.A: They are the only ones who can legally consent to treatment
options.B: They often recover despite receiving incorrect medications or treatments
options.C: They have the most specialized knowledge about their own diseases
options.D: They are responsible for reporting all clinical errors to the authorities
options.E: They provide the financial resources that sustain the healthcare system

questions.question: Which communication technique involves the recipient repeating back an order to ensure it was understood correctly by the sender?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because check-back is a technique where the sender initiates a message, the recipient provides feedback, and the sender double-checks for understanding. Option A is incorrect because call-out is used to inform all team members at once during emergencies. Option B is incorrect because ISBAR is a framework for organizing clinical information. Option C is incorrect because hand-over is the transfer of responsibility. Option D is incorrect because "I pass the baton" is a specific strategy for hand-offs.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: In its simplest form, what is the primary goal of human factors engineering in a clinical setting?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the study of human factors is defined as the study of all factors that make it easier to do work correctly, focusing on the fit between people and their environment. Option A is incorrect because human factors science acknowledges that human fallibility is universal and cannot be entirely eliminated. Option C is incorrect because human factors focuses on system design rather than profiling individuals for blame. Option D is incorrect because while technology is a tool, the goal is to optimize the relationship between humans and technology, not necessarily to replace humans. Option E is incorrect because increasing speed often leads to more errors; human factors aims for safety and efficiency, not just raw speed.

options.A: To eliminate the possibility of any human error occurring.
options.B: To make it easier for healthcare providers to do the work in the right way.
options.C: To identify which individuals are most prone to making mistakes.
options.D: To replace human decision-making with automated technological systems.
options.E: To increase the speed at which healthcare professionals perform tasks.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A hospital manager believes that if staff are sufficiently trained, work hard enough, and care about their patients, all medical errors can be avoided. Which model of human performance does this reflect?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the "perfectibility" model assumes that errors can be avoided through sufficient hard work, training, and caring, which is a traditional but flawed approach. Option A is incorrect because the systems model focuses on organizational design rather than individual effort. Option B is incorrect because High Reliability Organizations focus on preoccupation with failure and resilience. Option C is incorrect because the human factors model accepts that humans are fallible regardless of training or intent. Option E is incorrect because the resilience model focuses on the ability of a system to recover from unexpected threats.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A medical student is interrupted by a patient in a neighboring bed while they are labeling blood tubes. They assist the patient but subsequently forget to finish the labeling. This scenario demonstrates that human distractibility is:
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because human factors research shows that distractibility is a strength (helping us notice unusual events) and a weakness (causing us to lose focus on the primary task). Option B is incorrect because distractibility is an inherent part of how the human brain processes information and cannot be "suppressed." Option C is incorrect because errors due to distraction happen to even the most committed and well-trained professionals. Option D is incorrect because while fatigue increases the risk, distraction is a baseline human characteristic. Option E is incorrect because distractibility affects all humans, regardless of their level of experience.

options.A: Both a strength that allows for situational awareness and a weakness that predisposes to error.
options.B: A purely negative trait that must be suppressed through intense clinical vigilance.
options.C: A sign of inadequate professional training and lack of commitment to the task.
options.D: An indicator that the student is suffering from significant clinical fatigue.
options.E: A physiological response that only occurs in inexperienced healthcare learners.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: According to the TeamSTEPPS framework, which specific team type is primarily responsible for managing the environment, assets, and logistics to support the core clinical team?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because Support Services teams manage the environment, assets, and logistics to create an efficient and safe healthcare environment. Option A is incorrect because contingency teams are formed for specific emergent events like cardiac arrests. Option B is incorrect because coordinating teams are responsible for day-to-day operational management and resource coordination. Option C is incorrect because administration provides executive leadership and establishes the overall culture. Option D is incorrect because core teams consist of members directly involved in the immediate care of the patient.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Analysis of the 'Challenger' shuttle disaster by sociologist Diane Vaughan revealed that experts often negotiate risk factors until risky assessments become accepted as standard practice. What term describes this phenomenon?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because "normalization of deviance" (or normalization of risky assessments) describes the process where violations or risks become the accepted rule rather than the exception. Option A is incorrect because hindsight bias is the tendency to be "wise after the event" when judging others' mistakes. Option B is incorrect because active failures are the actual errors made by frontline workers. Option D is incorrect because latent conditions are the underlying system flaws like poor design or inadequate staffing. Option E is incorrect because an optimizing violation involves a person being motivated by personal goals like greed or thrills.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A clinician performs an experimental treatment on a patient primarily to achieve a personal goal of being the first in their field to publish the results, rather than following the standard protocol. This is categorized as which type of violation?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because optimizing violations occur when a person is motivated by personal goals, such as prestige, greed, or thrills, rather than the task at hand. Option A is incorrect because routine violations are common, tolerated deviations like failing to wash hands due to perceived business. Option C is incorrect because necessary violations are those made to get a job done in a time-poor or resource-poor environment. Option D is incorrect because "systemic violation" is not a standard category in Reason's violation taxonomy. Option E is incorrect because a violation is a deliberate deviation, whereas a knowledge-based error is an unintentional mistake due to lack of information.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A nurse administers the wrong drug. To find the root cause, the investigating team asks "Why?" repeatedly until they discover that the surgeon was too tired to write clearly, which was due to a 36-hour shift. This technique is known as:
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the "Five Whys" is a method used to keep discussions focused on the system by repeatedly asking why an event occurred to uncover underlying factors. Option A is incorrect because the DESC script is used for conflict resolution. Option B is incorrect because the Two-Challenge Rule is used to stop a safety breach. Option C is incorrect because ISBAR is a structured communication tool for clinical handovers. Option E is incorrect because "I PASS the BATON" is a strategy specifically for hand-offs.

options.E: The I PASS the BATON Tool

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: When using the "Two-Challenge Rule" to advocate for patient safety, what is the required first step if a team member notices a potential safety breach?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the Two-Challenge Rule dictates that the first challenge should be phrased as a question to the team member. Option B is incorrect because administrative notification is a much later step if the chain of command fails. Option C is incorrect because providing supporting data is the second step of the challenge. Option D is incorrect because CUS is a separate, though related, communication tool. Option E is incorrect because documentation occurs after the conflict is resolved or escalated, not as the first step to stop a breach.

options.A: State the concern in the form of a question.
options.C: Provide data to support the concern.
options.D: Use the CUS (Concerned, Uncomfortable, Safety) acronym.
options.E: Document the disagreement in the patient's chart.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A doctor orders a specific dose of medication over the phone to a nurse. The nurse repeats the dose back to the doctor, and the doctor confirms the nurse heard it correctly. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because a check-back is a closed-loop communication technique where the receiver accepts the message and provides feedback, and the sender double-checks the understanding. Option A is incorrect because a call-out is used to inform all team members simultaneously during an emergency. Option B is incorrect because ISBAR is a framework for communicating critical information about a patient's condition. Option D is incorrect because a handoff is the transfer of professional responsibility. Option E is incorrect because while this is a form of redundancy, "check-back" is the specific clinical communication term.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Following a serious medical error that results in patient harm, the healthcare professional involved often experiences significant emotional distress, guilt, and fear. According to Albert Wu, this individual is known as the:
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because Albert Wu coined the term "second victim" to describe healthcare workers who are traumatized by an error they were involved in. Option A is incorrect because latent failures are system-level flaws. Option C is incorrect because an active error is the action itself, not the person. Option D is incorrect because a sentinel event is the adverse outcome (serious injury or death). Option E is incorrect because a root cause is the underlying reason for the failure.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: In James Reason’s model of system safety, what principle involves the use of successive layers of protection—such as alarms, physical barriers, and containment—to guard against the failure of an underlying layer?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because "defence in-depth" is the principle where successive layers of protection are designed to guard against the failure of the underlying layer. Option A is incorrect because the Swiss Cheese model is the visual representation of how these layers have "holes" (failures). Option B is incorrect because resilience is the ability to proactively seek and contain threats. Option C is incorrect because the human-machine interface refers to the design of equipment for human use. Option E is incorrect because sensitivity to operations is a characteristic of High Reliability Organizations focusing on frontline issues.

questions.question: What is the WHO International Classification for Patient Safety term for unexpected harm resulting from a justified action where the correct process was followed?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because an adverse reaction is defined by the WHO as unexpected harm resulting from a justified action where the correct process was followed for the specific context. Option A is incorrect because a near miss is an incident that did not reach the patient. Option B is incorrect because a side-effect is a known, expected effect other than the primarily intended one, related to the pharmacological properties of a drug. Option C is incorrect because a sentinel event is a specific category of serious adverse events involving death or serious physical/psychological injury. Option E is incorrect because a violation is a deliberate deviation from an operating procedure or rule.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Human factors engineering (ergonomics) is best defined as the study of the interrelationship between which three components?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because human factors is defined as the study of the interrelationships between humans, the tools and equipment they use, and the environment in which they work. Option A is incorrect as it only lists specific personnel rather than the broader system components. Option B is incorrect because while it involves people, it misses the technological and environmental interfaces. Option D is incorrect as these are components of professional competence, not the definition of ergonomics. Option E is incorrect because these relate to the basic sciences of medicine rather than the science of safety and human performance.

options.B: Patients, their families, and the healthcare team
options.C: Humans, the tools they use, and their working environment
options.D: Medical knowledge, clinical skills, and professional ethics
options.E: Biological systems, pharmacological agents, and disease processes

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A medical student is asked to assist in a complex surgery but realizes they are currently taking a sedating antihistamine for hay fever. According to the "IM SAFE" self-assessment tool, which category does this concern fall under?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the "IM SAFE" tool uses "M" to represent Medication, prompting healthcare workers to evaluate if any drugs they are taking might impair their performance. Option A is incorrect as "Illness" refers to the underlying symptoms of the hay fever itself, rather than the side effects of the treatment. Option C is incorrect as "Stress" refers to psychological pressures. Option D is incorrect as "Alcohol" is a separate category in the mnemonic. Option E is incorrect as "Emotion" refers to the psychological state of the practitioner.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Research into human performance suggests that being awake for 24 hours causes a decline in clinical performance equivalent to having which blood alcohol concentration (BAC)?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because studies have shown that prolonged wakefulness (approximately 24 hours) produces a deterioration in performance equivalent to a blood alcohol level of 0.05%, which is the legal driving limit in many countries. Option A is incorrect as this level of impairment is too low to match 24 hours of sleep deprivation. Option B is incorrect as it underestimates the significant impairment caused by fatigue. Option C is incorrect as it is below the researched equivalence level. Option E is incorrect as 0.10% represents a much higher level of intoxication than typically seen with 24-hour fatigue.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A nurse accidentally administers a concentrated electrolyte solution because the vial looked identical to a normal saline vial and both were stored in the same drawer. In the context of human factors, the identical packaging and shared storage location are examples of:
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because latent conditions are systemic flaws (like poor design or storage) that exist long before an incident and "wait" for an active failure to occur. Option B is incorrect because the active failure is the specific act of the nurse picking up the wrong vial. Option C is incorrect as a routine violation is a deliberate deviation from a rule, which is not described here. Option D is incorrect as optimizing violations involve deviations for personal gain or thrill. Option E is incorrect as hindsight bias is the tendency of observers to see an event as predictable after it has occurred.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A surgical team is reviewing a case where a patient was harmed. They use a technique where they repeatedly ask "Why?" to move past the immediate human error and identify the underlying organizational flaws. What is the primary goal of this "Five Whys" technique?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the "Five Whys" is a root cause analysis tool designed to peel away layers of symptoms to find the actual systemic cause of a problem. Option A is incorrect as the tool is for safety improvement, not legal finger-pointing. Option B is incorrect because human factors focuses on fixing the system rather than punishing individuals. Option C is incorrect as the goal is to move away from a blame culture. Option D is incorrect because the technique seeks to find fixable system issues rather than dismissing errors as unavoidable.

options.A: To assign legal liability to the correct department
options.B: To determine which staff member requires disciplinary action
options.C: To satisfy the patient's family that someone has been blamed
options.D: To prove that the error was unavoidable due to patient complexity
options.E: To uncover the root causes of a system failure

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Human factors research indicates that healthcare workers are prone to error because the capacity of the "working memory" is limited. Approximately how many items can the average human brain hold in its short-term working memory?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because cognitive psychology suggests that the capacity of human working memory is limited to approximately 7 plus or minus 2 items (5 to 9 items). Option A is incorrect as it is an underestimate of normal cognitive capacity. Option C is incorrect as it exceeds the known limits of short-term working memory. Option D is incorrect as this would require significant cognitive "chunking" or long-term memory involvement. Option E is incorrect as human memory is biologically finite and easily overwhelmed.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: Which of the following is a core human factors principle used to reduce the likelihood of error during a patient hand-over?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because simplification and standardization are fundamental human factors strategies that reduce the cognitive load and the chance of miscommunication. Option A is incorrect as more people often increase the chance of information being lost or distorted. Option B is incorrect because relying on memory is a known high-risk factor for error. Option D is incorrect as time pressure is a major contributor to human error. Option E is incorrect as jargon can lead to misunderstandings; clear, objective language is preferred.

options.A: Increasing the number of staff involved in the transfer
options.B: Relying on the memory of the outgoing clinician
options.C: Simplifying and standardizing the communication process
options.D: Encouraging staff to work faster to save time
options.E: Using complex medical jargon to ensure precision

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A junior doctor is involved in a serious medical error that results in a patient's death. The doctor subsequently suffers from depression, feels isolated by colleagues, and contemplates leaving the profession. This scenario describes which phenomenon?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the "second victim" refers to healthcare providers who are involved in an unanticipated adverse patient event and become traumatized by the experience. Option A is incorrect as normalization of deviance refers to the gradual acceptance of unsafe practices. Option B is incorrect as hindsight bias relates to how others judge the error after the fact. Option C is incorrect as "blame and shame" is a cultural approach, whereas "second victim" describes the specific psychological impact on the provider. Option E is incorrect as authority gradient refers to the power balance within a team.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A hospital pharmacy decides to limit the variety of concentrations of Morphine available on the wards to only two standard strengths. This is an example of which human factors intervention?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because limiting options (constraints) and standardizing concentrations makes it harder to select the wrong dose, effectively "forcing" a safer choice. Option B is incorrect because human factors aims to reduce the need for constant vigilance, which is unsustainable. Option C is incorrect as it relates to team communication, not medication inventory. Option D is incorrect as "IM SAFE" is a personal self-assessment tool, not a system design strategy. Option E is incorrect as hindsight bias is a retrospective psychological tendency, not a prospective design intervention.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A medical student finds it difficult to concentrate on a patient's history because the ward is extremely noisy, the lighting is flickering, and there are constant interruptions. These factors are categorized as:
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because environmental factors include physical attributes of the workplace such as noise, lighting, and physical layout. Option A is incorrect as individual factors refer to the student's own state (e.g., fatigue). Option B is incorrect as task factors refer to the nature of the work itself (e.g., complexity of the history). Option C is incorrect as team factors refer to communication and roles. Option D is incorrect as organizational factors refer to high-level policies and culture.

questions.lo: F2-PS-002 Human factors and patient safety
questions.question: A senior consultant decides to use a new, unproven surgical technique primarily because they want to be the first in the country to perform it, despite the existence of a safer standard protocol. According to James Reason, this is an example of:
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because an optimizing violation occurs when a person deviates from a rule to satisfy a personal goal, such as prestige, greed, or thrill-seeking. Option A is incorrect as routine violations are "cutting corners" that become common practice. Option C is incorrect as necessary violations are done to get the job done under system pressure.
